title: Generating Training Data with Language Models: Towards Zero-Shot Language Understanding

structured.summary: The paper introduces 'SuperGen,' a method using a unidirectional language model to generate training data for fine-tuning a bidirectional model, aiming at zero-shot language understanding. It achieves competitive results on GLUE benchmarks compared to few-shot settings without using task-specific data, employing quality data selection and regularization techniques.

strengths.point: Innovative use of PLMs for zero-shot learning.
strengths.evidence: The approach leverages unidirectional PLMs to generate training data and achieves strong performance on GLUE tasks without task-specific data.
SuperGen uses generated data instead of relying on annotated datasets, showcasing its utility in scenarios with limited resources.

weaknesses.point: Limited novelty due to similar previous methods.
weaknesses.evidence: Text generation for data scarcity is discussed in related work (lines 55-79), indicating prior exploration of similar methods.
Results section (lines 13-16, 49-51) only reports accuracy, omitting metrics like F1 scores that would give more rounded evaluation insights.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient detail in methodology for data selection.
weaknesses.evidence: Selection based on probability is discussed (lines 52-54) without exploring other quality factors such as coherence or diversity.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in prompt crafting and regularization impact.
weaknesses.evidence: Method section (lines 115-126) summaries crafting without concrete examples or clarity on regularization effects (lines 52-53).

questions.question: How does the approach perform on tasks outside the GLUE benchmark?
questions.motivation: Understanding the generalizability of the method to other datasets or tasks is vital for assessing its practical applications.
